candidates.title: MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
candidates.reasoning: Use HRSC-derived DTMs to quantify regional slopes, drainage/basin morphology, and stratigraphic/geomorphic context needed to distinguish different fluvial depositional styles (e.g., channel belts vs. fans vs. deltas) across Arabia Terra.

candidates.title: MARS EXPRESS HRSC DATA
candidates.reasoning: Map-projected HRSC image products support regional geomorphic mapping of fluvial landforms and deposit geometries across Arabia Terra (useful where very-high-resolution images are not required or to provide regional continuity).

candidates.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES
candidates.reasoning: MOLA Mission Experiment Gridded Data Record (MEGDR) provides global/regional gridded topography for Arabia Terra—needed to assess paleoslopes, basin connectivity, and longitudinal profiles that bear on whether deposits reflect one or multiple fluvial systems.

candidates.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER IEGDR ARCHIVE
candidates.reasoning: Interpolated gridded MOLA topography products can be used alongside MEGDR to extract consistent elevation/slope surfaces for geomorphic comparisons among different Arabia Terra deposit assemblages.

candidates.title: MRO CRISM OBSERVATIONS
candidates.reasoning: CRISM targeted RDRs provide calibrated surface reflectance spectra at specific locations in Arabia Terra, enabling mineralogic facies mapping (e.g., clays/sulfates) that helps discriminate depositional environments and potentially different fluvial depositional systems.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MRDR Bundle
candidates.reasoning: CRISM MRDR (map-projected reduced data) is ideal for regional compositional mapping and correlation of mineral assemblages with geomorphic units, supporting tests of whether Arabia Terra deposits represent a single fluvial style or multiple depositional systems.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM DDR Bundle - 1001
candidates.reasoning: CRISM DDRs (derived geometry/ancillary products) are commonly used with CRISM RDR/MRDR to properly georeference and correct observations when comparing deposit types and distributions across Arabia Terra.

tool_input.summary: On the PDS Geosciences node, the most directly relevant datasets for evaluating fluvial depositional diversity in Arabia Terra are (1) regional topography from MGS MOLA gridded products (MEGDR/IEGDR), (2) regional imaging + DTMs from MEX HRSC REFDR map-projected products and DTMs, and (3) compositional mapping from MRO CRISM RDR/MRDR plus supporting DDR geometry/ancillary products. (Very high-resolution CTX/HiRISE image datasets are typically hosted outside the GEO node.)

tool_calls.tool_output: Final result processed.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Geosciences node at https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/.

Directory layout:
  mission/ → dataset_or_bundle/ → volume/ (PDS3) or sub-collections (PDS4)

Top-level mission directories: m2020, insight, msl, mro, mer, mex, ody, phx, mgs, mpf, viking, mariner, mars, mgn, premgn, venus, messenger, grail, clps, lunar, lro, earth, lab, near.

Common mission/instrument abbreviations:
  Mars Express = MEX (instruments: HRSC, OMEGA, MARSIS, PFS, SPICAM, MaRS)
  Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter = MRO (instruments: HiRISE, CTX, CRISM, SHARAD, MCS)
  Mars Science Laboratory / Curiosity = MSL (instruments: ChemCam, APXS, CheMin, SAM, Mastcam, MAHLI, DAN)
  Mars 2020 / Perseverance = M2020 (instruments: PIXL, SHERLOC, Mastcam-Z, SuperCam, RIMFAX)
  Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit=MER2, Opportunity=MER1) (instruments: Pancam, Mini-TES/MTES, APXS, MB, MI)
  Mars Global Surveyor = MGS (instruments: MOC, MOLA, TES, MAG)
  Mars Odyssey = ODY (instruments: THEMIS, GRS, NS)
  Phoenix = PHX (instruments: TEGA, MECA, SSI, OM, RAC)
  Viking = VL1/VL2/VO1/VO2 (instruments: camera, IRTM, MAWD)
  MESSENGER = MESS (instruments: MDIS, GRNS, XRS, MLA, MASCS)
  Magellan = MGN (instruments: SAR, altimetry, radiometry, emissivity)
  LRO (instruments: LOLA, Diviner, LROC, Mini-RF, LAMP)
  GRAIL (instruments: LGRS)
  NEAR (instruments: NLR, MSI, XGRS, MAG)

PDS3 vs PDS4: both formats co-exist on the same node. PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style identifiers; PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`.

Bundle vs collection (PDS4): a bundle directory holds the bundle label plus sub-directories containing collection labels. `inspect_with_collections` returns both levels.

Extracting dataset_id from labels:
  - PDS3: VOLUME.DATA_SET_ID
  - PDS4 bundle: Identification_Area.logical_identifier (in `labels`)
  - PDS4 collection: Identification_Area.logical_identifier (in `collections`)

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires you to return dataset candidates. Queries come from published scientific papers and always imply specific PDS datasets that the researchers used. NEVER dismiss a query as 'interpretation', 'conceptual', or 'not a dataset question'. Even if the question sounds abstract or science-focused, your job is to identify which PDS datasets would be needed to answer it. Always return at least one candidate.

  2. When search results return a high-scoring match (score >= 80), strongly prefer it as a candidate. Do NOT ignore high-score results in favor of lower-scoring or unrelated datasets.

  3. Prefer CALIBRATED and REDUCED data products over raw/EDR data when the query implies scientific analysis. Scientists typically use calibrated (RDR, CDR) or derived (DDR) products.
